title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a project to restore biodiversity in their cities. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Wild Cities: Bringing Nature Back to the Streets

texts.A:
### Sam, The Rooftop Garden Initiative
'I used to see rooftops as dead space, but now I see potential,' Sam says. [51]The most challenging aspect was the logistical nightmare of transporting tons of soil to the top of a twenty-story building without a service lift.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my hay fever symptoms actually decreased, as if my body was adapting to the local flora.[/48] [53]I learned that nature is incredibly resilient; even in the heart of the city, life finds a way if given a small chance.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in structural engineering was essential for ensuring the roof could handle the weight of the wet soil.[/50] [47]Initially, the constant exposure to high winds at that altitude was a shock to my system.[/47] It was a rewarding high-altitude mission.
texts.B:
### Elena, The Urban Canal Cleanup
Elena spent her weekends removing debris from a neglected city waterway. 'It was a lesson in hidden history,' she recalls. [47]I hadn't expected the psychological toll of seeing the sheer amount of household waste dumped in the water to affect me so much.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the shared passion of the local residents, who brought us hot tea and words of encouragement.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of peace that comes from seeing a kingfisher return to a stretch of water you've cleaned.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that our urban environments are far more fragile than their concrete exteriors suggest.[/49] [56]If I hadn't seen a call for volunteers in a local community newspaper, I’d never have known about the project.[/56] It was a transformative period.
texts.C:
### Marcus, The Pocket Park Project
Marcus helped convert a derelict parking lot into a community garden. 'It’s about social glue,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the bureaucratic hurdles and the skepticism of some neighbors who feared the park would attract noise.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general fitness and stamina improved significantly, likely due to the constant digging and planting.[/48] [53]I realized that green spaces are not a luxury, but a fundamental necessity for mental health in a city.[/53] [50]I found that the communication skills from my job as a mediator were perfect for resolving conflicts between different volunteer groups.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the physical grime and the smell of the initial site, which I found quite repulsive.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried it wouldn't be big enough to make a difference.
texts.D:
### Sophie, The Native Wildflower Verge
'I wanted to replace boring grass with life,' Sophie says. [49]The project made me realize that our aesthetic obsession with 'tidy' gardens is actually harmful to local insect populations.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have known which seeds to plant if a local botanist hadn't provided a detailed guide for the area.[/56] [54]I was moved by the curiosity of school children who stopped to ask about the different types of bees we were attracting.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of manual weeding to be so constant and draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial weeks of waiting for the first sprouts to appear incredibly anxious and frustrating.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that the local council would stop us, but they actually became our biggest supporters.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the project's specific location?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions the wind at high altitude, Elena (B) the toll of seeing waste, and Sophie (D) the exhaustion of weeding.

questions.question: notes an unexpected positive change in their physical health or fitness?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) saw his hay fever symptoms decrease, and Marcus (C) saw his general fitness improve.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on urban fragility or modern aesthetic standards?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) reflects on the fragility of urban environments, and Sophie (D) on the harm of 'tidy' gardens.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the restoration?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) used structural engineering, and Marcus (C) used his skills as a mediator.

questions.question: describes a logistical or social challenge that made the project difficult to advance?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions the lack of a service lift, and Marcus (C) mentions bureaucratic hurdles/skeptical neighbors.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having an unexpected emotional response to seeing wildlife?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) felt peace seeing a kingfisher return, and Marcus (C) worried the park would be too small.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about nature's resilience or human necessity?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) learned about nature's resilience, and Marcus (C) about green spaces being a necessity.

questions.question: highlights the value of the support or interest shown by the local community?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) mentions residents bringing tea, and Sophie (D) mentions the curiosity of school children.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the unpleasant or slow aspects of the task?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) adjusted to the grime/smell, and Sophie (D) was anxious waiting for sprouts to appear.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific source of information or opportunity that enabled them to start?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) saw a community newspaper, and Sophie (D) was helped by a local botanist.
